architecture.summary: The paper uses Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) trained on model activations to interpret internal representations in language models. SAEs decompose residual stream activations into sparse linear combinations of learned dictionary elements. The system identifies specific SAE latent directions that activate selectively on known vs. unknown entities by analyzing activation patterns on the final token of entity mentions. These latent directions are then causally manipulated via activation steering to steer model behavior (refusal vs. hallucination). The paper also analyzes how these directions causally affect downstream attention mechanisms involved in attribute extraction.
trained_components: Sparse Autoencoders (pre-trained by Gemma Scope / LlamaScope, not trained in this work)

biblio.title: Do I Know This Entity? Knowledge Awareness and Hallucinations in Language Models

claims.statement: Using sparse autoencoders, SAE latent directions are discovered that encode knowledge awareness: detecting whether the model recognizes an entity and can recall facts about it

claims.dataset: Wikidata entities (basketball players, movies, cities, songs)
claims.statement: Entity recognition latents generalize across diverse entity types (players, films, songs, cities) with higher minimum separation scores in middle layers indicating entity-type-agnostic features
Steering with the unknown entity latent induces almost 100% refusal rate across all entity types in Gemma 2 2B when tested on 100 questions about unknown entities

Weight orthogonalization of the top unknown entity direction on every output matrix results in large reduction in refusal responses, demonstrating the direction plays a crucial role in knowledge refusal

Entity recognition latents causally regulate attention to entity tokens in downstream attention heads; steering with unknown entity latent reduces attention to entity even in prompts with known entities

claims.dataset: entity attribute questions (correct vs. incorrect responses)
claims.statement: An uncertainty latent in Gemma 2B IT layer 13 achieves 73.2 AUROC score and 72 F1 score as a classifier distinguishing correct versus incorrect responses on entity attribute questions
Despite SAEs being trained on the base model, entity recognition latent directions have causal effect on the chat model's refusal behavior, suggesting chat finetuning repurposes existing mechanisms

The methodology for labeling entities as 'known' or 'unknown' might introduce labeling inaccuracies, as the model could guess attributes despite not knowing about the entity or fail to recall specific attributes while knowing about the entity
